meta.name: CERQ-Short Cognitive Emotion Regulation Questionnaire
meta.description: The CERQ-Short is an 18-item brief version of the Cognitive Emotion Regulation Questionnaire, assessing 9 cognitive emotion regulation strategies used after experiencing negative events.
meta.author: Garnefski & Kraaij (2006)
meta.reference: Garnefski N, Kraaij V. Cognitive emotion regulation questionnaire - development of a short 18-item version (CERQ-short). Pers Individ Dif. 2006;41(6):1045-1053.
meta.instruction: Everyone gets confronted with negative or unpleasant events now and then. The following statements describe thoughts you might have after experiencing negative events. Please rate how often you generally think this way.

questions.text: I feel that I am the one who is responsible for what has happened
I think that I have to accept that this has happened
I often think about how I feel about what I have experienced
I think of pleasant things that have nothing to do with it
I think about what I can learn from the situation
I think that it has not been too bad compared to other things
I keep thinking about how terrible it is what I have experienced
I think about what is the best thing I can do
I feel that others are responsible for what has happened
I feel that I am the one to blame for it
I think that I have to accept the situation
I am preoccupied with what I think and feel about what I have experienced
I think of something nice instead of what has happened
I think that the situation also has its positive sides
I think that it could have been much worse
I continually think how horrible the situation has been
I think about a plan of what I can do best
I feel that others are to blame for it

self_blame.label: Low
self_blame.description: You rarely use self-blame as a cognitive strategy.

self_blame.label: Average
self_blame.description: You sometimes use self-blame, within the normal range.

self_blame.label: High
self_blame.description: You frequently use self-blame, a maladaptive strategy that may affect mental health when overused.

acceptance.label: Average
acceptance.description: You can moderately accept what has happened.

acceptance.label: High
acceptance.description: You are good at accepting unchangeable facts, an adaptive strategy.

rumination.label: Low
rumination.description: You rarely engage in ruminative thinking.

rumination.label: Average
rumination.description: You sometimes dwell on negative events.

rumination.label: High
rumination.description: You frequently ruminate, associated with increased depression risk.

positive_refocusing.label: Low
positive_refocusing.description: You rarely redirect attention to positive things.

positive_refocusing.label: Average
positive_refocusing.description: You sometimes redirect attention to positive things.

positive_refocusing.label: High
positive_refocusing.description: You are good at redirecting attention to pleasant things.

positive_reappraisal.label: Average
positive_reappraisal.description: You sometimes find positive meaning in negative events.

positive_reappraisal.label: High
positive_reappraisal.description: You are skilled at finding positive meaning, one of the most protective strategies.

putting_into_perspective.label: Low
putting_into_perspective.description: You rarely put events into perspective.

putting_into_perspective.label: Average
putting_into_perspective.description: You sometimes view events in a broader context.

putting_into_perspective.label: High
putting_into_perspective.description: You are good at putting events into broader perspective.

catastrophizing.label: High
catastrophizing.description: You frequently catastrophize, closely linked to anxiety and depression.

planning.label: Low
planning.description: You rarely think about how to improve situations.

planning.label: High
planning.description: You are skilled at making plans to improve situations.

other_blame.label: Average
other_blame.description: You sometimes attribute events to others.

other_blame.label: High
other_blame.description: You frequently blame others, a maladaptive strategy.
